bibliographic.citation: Emil Hausknecht, ed., The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone and of Ferumbras His Sone Who Conquerede Rome, The English Charlemagne Romances, Part V, EETS Extra Series 38 (London: N. Trubner & Co., 1881).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 38
bibliographic.title: The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone and of Ferumbras His Sone Who Conquerede Rome

entities.description: The immediate printed source of CME00053, newly edited from the unique late Sir Thomas Phillipps manuscript. The poem is on printed pp. 1-94.
entities.label: Hausknecht's 1881 Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone

rights.basis: Published in 1881; the source item is marked not in copyright by Internet Archive metadata.

bibliographic.citation: Emil Hausknecht, ed., The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone, later Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co. plate-derived issue of EETS Extra Series 38, cataloged as 1881; exact impression unresolved.

dateStatements.label: issue
dateStatements.notes: Internal evidence establishes that this is later than the original issue but does not securely identify the exact impression.

entities.description: A later issue preserving the 1881 pagination and text. Its title page names Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co.; the verso says the volume was reprinted in 1891 and 1898, so the scanned issue is not the original N. Trubner 1881 publication despite catalog metadata.
entities.label: Post-1898 plate-derived reprint of Hausknecht's edition, cataloged as 1881

bibliographic.citation: The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone and of Ferumbras His Sone Who Conquerede Rome (London: printed for the Roxburghe Club by William Nicol, Shakespeare Press, 1854).
bibliographic.publisher: Roxburghe Club; William Nicol, Shakespeare Press
bibliographic.title: The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone and of Ferumbras His Sone Who Conquerede Rome

entities.description: The first printed edition, copied from Sir Thomas Phillipps's manuscript and collated against the original during printing. The poem occupies printed pp. 1-118 and was printed without modern punctuation.
entities.label: Roxburghe Club 1854 Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone

rights.basis: Published in 1854; Internet Archive metadata marks the source item not in copyright.

entities.description: The unique Middle English witness of The Sowdone of Babylone, copied in the East Midlands around 1450. The poem fills ff. 1r-41r; f. 41v carries later ownership material.

holding.notes: Gift of Robert Garrett to Princeton University Library in 1942.

entities.label: Princeton University Library MS Garrett 140

physicalDescription.dimensions: 268 x 192 mm; text space 200 x 105 mm
physicalDescription.format: One column, 38-41 long lines, secretary script
notes: Late eighteenth-century quarter-calf binding with marbled-paper pasteboards, rebacked; spine title present.
Unexecuted initials and no decoration; a later-hand line occurs at f. 5r.

rights.basis: The text was composed and copied in the Middle Ages; U-M marks its CME item No Copyright - United States.

notes: No public digital facsimile was linked from the official record when checked; consultation or reproduction must be arranged through Bodleian Special Collections.

access.accessMethod: Use the Bodleian archives record and Special Collections contact routes to request consultation or reproduction information, quoting MS. Douce 175.

entities.description: George Steevens's late-eighteenth-century paper transcript of the unique Sowdone manuscript, given to Francis Douce in 1790. No public digital surrogate was found.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Douce 175

notes: The record reports legacy full digital coverage but says that digital images are currently unavailable. Zero target-text overlap: the relevant contents are source analogues, not The Sowdone of Babylone.

access.accessMethod: The official catalog's legacy digitization is not currently exposed; use the British Library digitized-manuscript request form to ask about restored access.

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: second quarter of the fourteenth century

entities.description: A fourteenth-century Anglo-Norman codex containing La Destruccion de Rome at ff. 64r-83r and Fierabras at ff. 84r-118v. These are source analogues, not the Middle English target text, and the codex has zero target-text overlap.
entities.label: British Library Egerton MS 3028

physicalDescription.extent: 118 leaves plus three front and three rear paper flyleaves
notes: The complete codex also contains an abridged Roman de Brut and continuation at ff. 1r-63r.

access.accessMethod: Browse the entire manuscript, use its IIIF Presentation manifest, or directly download the whole PDF.
access.entityLabel: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz Bibliothek Ms IV 578

localCopies.label: Complete 210-image GWLB provider sequence
notes: Zero target-text overlap: this manuscript does not contain The Sowdone of Babylone; its source analogues occupy La Destruction de Rome at ff. 1r-24v/canvases 5-52 and Fierabras at ff. 25r-100v/canvases 53-204.

All normal page/surface requests used valid IIIF width syntax 1800,; the narrow spine was retained at its native 620-pixel width and the color target at 1800 x 540. ZIP integrity and representative dimensions were audited.

The provider sequence includes four unlabelled opening surfaces, ff. 1r-100v, four unlabelled closing surfaces, a spine view, and a color target. No separate fore-edge, head-edge, or tail-edge photographs are exposed.

localCopies.label: Complete GWLB whole-manuscript PDF
notes: Zero target-text overlap: this manuscript does not contain The Sowdone of Babylone; its source analogues occupy PDF/canvas pages 5-52 and 53-204 for La Destruction de Rome and Fierabras respectively.
The full 210-page provider PDF is retained, including opening and closing surfaces, spine, and color target.

rights.basis: The official IIIF manifest marks the digital object with the Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0 and supplies attribution text.
notes: Preferred credit: Provided by Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz Bibliothek / No Copyright - Public Domain Marked.

entities.description: An Anglo-Norman source-analogue manuscript containing La Destruction de Rome at ff. 1r-24v and Fierabras at ff. 25r-100v. Hausknecht inspected it after drafting his introduction and discussed it in additions on pp. xlix-liv. It does not contain The Sowdone of Babylone and has zero target-text overlap.
entities.label: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz Bibliothek Ms IV 578

notes: Produced in East Anglia; the codex contains 103 miniatures according to scholarly descriptions.

next_steps: Treat Egerton MS 3028 and Hannover Ms IV 578 only as cognate source witnesses unless a stemmatic study identifies a direct exemplar.
Review recent source studies of La Destruction de Rome and Fierabras before assigning a more specific adaptation relationship.
openQuestions.question: Which lost or surviving Anglo-Norman version most directly underlies the Middle English poem?

next_steps: Ask Princeton Special Collections whether the complete cached facsimile may be redistributed in a public Git repository that can support commercial print-on-demand projects.
Until clarified, link to Princeton's official facsimile and use local files for research rather than publishing them.
openQuestions.question: How should Princeton's generated-PDF noncommercial/fair-use wording be reconciled with its current no-permission-fee publication policy for collection images?

next_steps: Submit the official digitized-manuscript request form, quoting Egerton MS 3028 and asking for the whole physical object rather than selected folios.
openQuestions.question: When will the British Library's legacy full digitization of Egerton MS 3028 become publicly accessible again, and is a whole-object download available by request?

next_steps: Compare a stratified sample of all 327 HI italic runs with Garrett MS 140 and Hausknecht's footnotes before encoding a global rendering rule.
openQuestions.question: Does every italic run in Hausknecht and CME00053 represent supplied expansion letters, or do italics serve additional functions?

next_steps: Compare publisher imprints and EETS issue records for the 1891, 1898, and later impressions; retain the original 1881 scan as the immediate edition surrogate.
openQuestions.question: What exact impression and year does the Toronto-scanned post-1898 plate-derived Hausknecht issue represent?

next_steps: Prefer current Princeton catalog data for display, preserve the attributed scholarly variant, and ask the repository only if exact codicological measurement is material.
openQuestions.question: Why do Sanchez-Marti's measurements and line counts differ from Princeton's current catalog?

reviewNotes: The whole-object rule is applied to every downloadable manuscript or edition: cached files retain all provider-exposed covers, endleaves, blanks, adjacent matter, and supporting texts rather than cropping to the poem.

Only Garrett MS 140 contains the Middle English target text. Hannover Ms IV 578 and BL Egerton MS 3028 are source analogues with zero target-text overlap and therefore receive no local-copy work_portion.
The 78 XML manuscript-side milestones reproduce Hausknecht's side/page numbering and are not current Princeton folio numbers; the reliable modern witness locator is ff. 1r-41r.

The cached Toronto scan cataloged as 1881 has an internal Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner title page and a verso noting reprints in 1891 and 1898; it is modeled separately from the original N. Trubner 1881 issue.
Underlying medieval text, editorial text, digital encoding, scanned files, and provider access or reuse terms are separate rights layers.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is copied from University of Michigan CME item CME00053, which encodes only the title page and the 3,274-line poem on pp. 1-94 of Emil Hausknecht's 1881 EETS edition. Hausknecht re-edited the poem directly from its unique Middle English witness, Princeton University Library MS Garrett 140 (formerly Phillipps MS 8357), ff. 1r-41r, while acknowledging and superseding the Roxburghe Club's first printing of 1854. Complete provider objects are cached for Garrett 140, both nineteenth-century editions, a later plate-derived Hausknecht reprint, and Hausknecht's directly consulted source analogue GWLB Ms IV 578. Garrett's full 88-canvas sequence maps the target to ff. 1r-41r, canvases 5-85. Hannover Ms IV 578 has zero target-text overlap: its Destruction de Rome and Fierabras occupy ff. 1r-100v and were consulted only as source analogues. British Library Egerton MS 3028 is another cognate Anglo-Norman witness, but its legacy images were unavailable when checked. The exact Anglo-Norman exemplar or version adapted by the anonymous Middle English poet remains unidentified.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English poet-adapter
catalogSummary.date: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; commonly dated around 1400
catalogSummary.title: The Sowdone of Babylone

catalogingSubject.label: The Sowdone of Babylone
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English Charlemagne romance represented by the unique text in Princeton University Library MS Garrett 140 and encoded in CME00053. This work-level record does not collapse the medieval poem, its lost or unknown Anglo-Norman source version, Garrett's physical codex, Hausknecht's 1881 edition, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML into a single object.

contentParts.label: The Sowdone of Babylone
notes: The source edition's title page is encoded separately as DIV1[1] but is paratext, not an additional content part of the medieval work.
contentParts.summary: The complete continuous romance from the opening appeal to God through the concluding prayer and colophon.

assertion.notes: This is a scholarly composition range, not the date of the surviving copy.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century, commonly around 1400
Circa 1450 in the unique surviving copy, Princeton MS Garrett 140
notes: The witness date does not establish the composition date.

dateStatements.display: First printed by the Roxburghe Club in 1854

extent.description: One continuous 3,274-line verse romance. The XML serializes the poem in two LG elements of 369 and 2,905 lines because line 370 is assigned to a later hand; those encoding groups are not separate work divisions.

formStatements.description: A 3,274-line verse romance, principally in four-line abab quatrains with some eight-line stanzas.

notes: The unique copy's circa-1450 date and East Midlands language are witness-level facts; the work's composition is earlier and remains broadly dated.
The XML's manuscript-side milestones reproduce Hausknecht's manuscript pagination and should not be presented as current Princeton foliation.
Hannover Ms IV 578 and BL Egerton MS 3028 contain only source analogues and have zero target-text overlap.
Religious-conflict and anti-Muslim subject terms describe historically situated content and rhetoric, not modern endorsement.
Hausknecht is displayed as editor because CME00053 represents his edition, while the medieval work remains anonymously authored.

next_steps: Review current linguistic, historical, and source scholarship before replacing the broad 1375-1425 normalization with a narrower date.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's broad late-fourteenth-/early-fifteenth-century composition range be narrowed securely?

next_steps: Keep the abstract lost/unknown source distinct from Egerton MS 3028 and Hannover Ms IV 578; consult recent stemmatic work on La Destruction de Rome and Fierabras.
openQuestions.question: What exact lost or surviving Anglo-Norman version did the Middle English poet adapt?

next_steps: Retain the current Database of Middle English Romance value 950 in the main identifier and add 950.1 only if an authoritative index record establishes its scope.
openQuestions.question: Should the identifier be displayed as IMEV 950 or the more granular 950.1 used in some scholarship?

assertion.notes: Database classification is preferred as a qualified regional attribution rather than an exact place.

notes: The dialect of the circa-1450 copy is not automatically identical to the author's original dialect.

Major setting and object of attack and conquest in the narrative
Realm and campaign geography associated with Charlemagne and his peers
Campaign geography of the Ferumbras tradition

assertion.notes: The broad source traditions are supported, but the exact Anglo-Norman version or physical exemplar is not identified.

scope.description: Adaptation and combination of Anglo-Norman Destruction de Rome and Fierabras traditions
Immediate 1881 source-edition representation, not medieval authorship

subjects.label: Anti-Muslim polemic and religious violence
notes: The catalog term describes the poem's hostile conventional representation of Muslims and coerced or violent conversion; it does not endorse that representation.

scope.description: Ferumbras's conversion and conflict with his father, Laban

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English Charlemagne romance that combines the destruction-of-Rome and Fierabras traditions. After the Sultan Laban's forces attack Rome and carry off relics, Charlemagne's campaigns bring violent conflict, sieges, the capture and rescue of the Twelve Peers, and the conversion of Ferumbras and Floripas. The poem frames Christian victory and Saracen defeat through strongly polemical religious and chivalric conventions.

The work survives uniquely in Princeton MS Garrett 140, ff. 1r-41r. CME00053 follows Hausknecht's 1881 edition and contains 3,274 verse lines. Its two XML LG elements reflect an encoding split around a line added by a later hand at line 370/f. 5r, not two independent intellectual parts.

notes: A conventional scholarly title normalized to Hausknecht's spelling; the unique manuscript has no formal title page.
Modernized title used by the Database of Middle English Romance and Lupack's edition.
An editorial title, not a securely attested medieval rubric.

scope.description: Long title of the 1854 and 1881 printed editions

titles.value: The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone and of Ferumbras His Sone Who Conquerede Rome

work.author: Anonymous Middle English poet-adapter
work.dateDisplay: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; commonly dated around 1400
work.summary: An anonymous Middle English Charlemagne romance that combines the destruction-of-Rome and Fierabras traditions. After the Sultan Laban's forces attack Rome and carry off relics, Charlemagne's campaigns bring violent conflict, sieges, the capture and rescue of the Twelve Peers, and the conversion of Ferumbras and Floripas. The poem frames Christian victory and Saracen defeat through strongly polemical religious and chivalric conventions.
